frameworks.name: Reframing Through Narrative
frameworks.definition: Changing the linguistic and contextual frame applied to a situation alters human emotional and perceptual response to that situation, without changing the objective facts.

claims.text: Reframing the context and narrative of a situation changes its meaning and emotional weight, even when the objective facts remain unchanged. Telling a different story about the same reality produces a different emotional and behavioral response.
claims.speaker: Unknown narrator
claims.mechanism: Human attention and narrative interpretation are the primary drivers of emotional response; the objective situation is secondary to the story told about it.
bounded_by: Does not change the objective reality or facts of the situation
Reframing is effective only when the alternative frame is coherent and plausible within the same facts

examples.text: An airport bus service is universally perceived as an inconvenience—passengers expect an air bridge to the terminal and feel cheated when the bus arrives instead. A pilot at Gatwick airport reframed the bus by announcing: "The good news is the bus will take you all the way to passport control so you won't have far to walk with your bags." This is objectively always true, but the new frame inverted the meaning: the bus became a conveyance that saved passengers from walking past retail stands with luggage, rather than an inferior alternative to an air bridge. The same situation, told through a different narrative, shifted from negative to positive.

practices.text: When presenting a situation or outcome to an audience, lead with the positive reframe before revealing the constraint. Anchor the narrative to benefit-relevant facts the audience will experience, not to what they will lose.
